correctness.rationale: Core choices (time-respecting cross-fitting, embargoed walk-forward, mediator exclusions, FDR) are sound with only minor methodological leaks and alignment nits remaining.

robustness.rationale: Good gating on low-N, missingness handling, HAC SEs, and diagnostics; a few spots (scaling leakage, off-by-one residual join) reduce resilience under edge cases.

scalability.rationale: Pipeline is vectorized and incremental enough for 10× n; causal bootstraps are the main O(n·B) cost but fine at current scale.

idiomaticness.rationale: Uses standard libraries and patterns (sklearn, statsmodels, Prophet, XGBoost, BH-FDR) in a way an experienced reviewer would recognize.

response.summary:
This pipeline has matured into a statistically careful, time-respecting analysis for a single-user longitudinal setting. High points include: behavioral-day attribution end to end; rolling features that exclude the target row; an embargoed multi-horizon walk-forward backtest; SARIMAX exogenous shifting validated by checks; AIPW with TimeSeriesSplit to respect autocorrelation; and multiple-comparison control extended to the large journal and habit families. The causal layer correctly excludes mediators and limits confounders to pre-treatment lags, reports AIPW/PSM/naive side-by-side, and adds E-values, which is appropriate for a self-experiment context.

Remaining risks are mostly second-order but worth addressing: (1) feature standardization for AIPW/PSM is fit on the full sample before folds, which leaks future-fold distributional information into the training step; (2) the error-mode residual breakdown joins residuals to the prediction date’s journal/habit flags rather than the feature day (off by one), potentially misleading post-hoc narratives; and (3) nominal prediction-interval levels differ across models (XGBoost/SARIMAX at 90% vs Prophet’s 80%), making the stored ci_coverage metric not directly comparable. Granger’s per-lag multiplicity and PSM’s lack of a caliper are lesser issues.

If I could fix only three things: (1) fit the StandardScaler within each cross-fitting fold and in PSM’s propensity step to remove leakage; (2) realign the error-mode joins to use feature-date (prediction_date − 1 for h=1) and document the convention; (3) standardize PI nominal coverage across models (or store per-row nominal_level) so ci_coverage is interpretable across methods and horizons.

findings.title: AIPW/PSM standardization fit on full sample leaks future-fold info
findings.description: Both AIPW and PSM call _standardize(X) once on the full dataset before splitting, so fold-specific models are trained on features centered/scaled using information from the validation folds (future in time), violating strict time-respecting cross-fitting.
findings.evidence: def _standardize(X): scaler = StandardScaler(); return scaler.fit_transform(X). In estimate_aipw: X_std = _standardize(X); kf = TimeSeriesSplit(...). In estimate_psm: X_std = _standardize(X) precedes fitting the propensity on all rows.
findings.recommendation: Within each fold, fit the scaler on Xt and transform Xt/Xv separately; for PSM, fit the scaler on all rows used in the propensity model within the trimmed, time-respecting analysis subset or refit per fold when doing cross-fit AIPW. Implement a fold-aware scaler to avoid leakage.

findings.title: Error-mode residuals joined to wrong day (off-by-one with journal/habit)
findings.description: Residuals for h=1 predictions are merged to journal_/habit_ flags on prediction_date, but the model’s features (and behaviors of interest) are from the prior day, so the breakdown attributes errors to the wrong day’s behaviors.
findings.evidence: xgb_bt['pred_date'] = prediction_date; joined = xgb_bt.merge(jdf, left_on='pred_date', right_on='calendar_date', ...). For h=1, features for date D produce a prediction with prediction_date D+1.
findings.recommendation: Join on feature-date for h=1 (prediction_date minus 1 day) and, more generally, prediction_date minus h for horizon h. Add a small helper to compute feature_date = prediction_date − horizon_days.

findings.title: Nominal PI levels inconsistent across models; ci_coverage not comparable
findings.description: XGBoost and SARIMAX use ±1.645σ (≈90%) while Prophet uses its default ~80% bands; the schema comment refers to 80%. Mixing nominal levels makes ci_coverage incomparable across models/horizons.
findings.evidence: XGB: test_lower = pred − 1.645*pred_std; SARIMAX: conf_int(alpha=0.10) → 90%; Prophet uses yhat_lower/yhat_upper (80% default) and is labeled '80% CI' in plotting.
findings.recommendation: Standardize to a single nominal level (e.g., 80% or 90%) for all models, or add a 'ci_nominal' field in hrv_model_metrics and compute coverage against that level per row.

findings.title: Prophet holdout uses realized regressors, inflating reported skill
findings.description: The 30-day holdout evaluation merges actual regressor values from the holdout period and forward-fills, which evaluates conditional accuracy given known future exogenous inputs rather than a genuine forecast scenario.
findings.evidence: future_holdout = m.make_future_dataframe(...); then for rf in reg_feats: future_holdout = future_holdout.merge(hrv_df[['ds', rf]], ...).ffill().
findings.recommendation: Either (a) treat Prophet’s reported metrics as conditional-on-exog and label them as such, or (b) simulate exogenous features for the holdout (e.g., last-values or seasonal baselines) to reflect true forecasting difficulty.

findings.title: Granger per-lag multiplicity not adjusted; may overstate significance
findings.description: Granger is run for lags 1..3 and p-values are stored without adjustment, so per-feature multiple testing across lags can inflate false positives even though inputs are FDR survivors.
findings.evidence: gc = grangercausalitytests(..., maxlag=3); loop over lag in 1..3 appends p_value with no correction.
findings.recommendation: Within each feature, adjust p-values across tested lags (e.g., Holm/Bonferroni) or report the minimum p with a note about the 3-lag family-wise error.

findings.title: PSM lacks caliper; poor matches under propensity-model misspecification
findings.description: Nearest-neighbor matching on logit propensity has no caliper, so treated units can be matched to controls with materially different propensities under model misspecification, inflating bias/variance.
findings.evidence: dists = |logit_p[control_idx] − logit_p[ti]|; takes k smallest without a maximum allowed distance.
findings.recommendation: Add a caliper (e.g., 0.2·SD of logit propensity) and drop treated units with no control within the caliper; record and report caliper drops.

findings.title: Fold-level class imbalance may break AIPW folds silently
findings.description: When a training fold lacks either class, psi[test_idx] is set to NaN and the fold is skipped; with sparse treatments this can affect many folds without alerting the user.
findings.evidence: if Tt.sum() < 2 or (Tt==0).sum() < 2: psi[test_idx] = np.nan; continue; later uses psi_valid with no per-treatment warning.
findings.recommendation: Log per-treatment fold drop counts and, if too many folds fail (e.g. >2 of 5), flag the estimate as unreliable in the output.

things_done_well.title: AIPW cross-fitting uses TimeSeriesSplit on autocorrelated HRV
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Time-respecting folds avoid outcome leakage across adjacent days, a common pitfall in doubly-robust estimation on time series.

things_done_well.title: Exogenous regressor shift for SARIMAX is explicit and validated
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Shifting exog by one day with a contract check prevents subtle ‘same-day’ leakage and guarantees alignment between behaviors and the HRV they aim to forecast.

things_done_well.title: Embargo gap in walk-forward prevents rolling-window leakage
things_done_well.why_it_matters: A 28-day gap matches the longest rolling feature window, ensuring training data cannot indirectly contain information about the first test rows.

things_done_well.title: Rolling HRV baselines exclude the target row
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Shifting by one before computing rolling means/stds avoids target leakage that would otherwise inflate feature–target associations.

things_done_well.title: BH-FDR applied to journal and habit families
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Corrects the largest multiple-comparison surface (59+ journal questions), materially reducing false-positive behavioral ‘hits’.

things_done_well.title: Stage-3 OLS uses HAC SEs and VIF-based pruning
things_done_well.why_it_matters: HAC standard errors acknowledge serial correlation, and VIF pruning tames multicollinearity, making standardized betas more interpretable.

things_done_well.title: Prediction intervals based on out-of-fold residuals
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Using OOF residuals for σ produces more realistic PI coverage than in-sample residuals, improving risk calibration.

questions_for_followup: Schema vs code disagreement: hrv_model_metrics.ci_coverage is documented as 80% in SCHEMA.md; should all models standardize to 80% or should we store a ci_nominal column? Please confirm the desired nominal level.
For causal AIPW, do we want per-treatment logs of folds dropped for class imbalance (to expose fragile estimates in the UI)? If yes, where should this surface in hrv_analysis_results?
Are Prophet metrics intended to be ‘conditional on realized exogenous inputs’ or should we simulate exogenous features for a true forecast evaluation? Clarify the evaluation contract.

Confirm that journal_* coverage semantics should exclude pre-tracking days (current code does so by dropna on treatment); do we want to fill 0 only from the first-asked date onward for journals, like habits/supplements?
Do we want to harmonize Granger reporting by adjusting per-lag multiplicity (Bonferroni/Holm) or only show the best lag with a correction note?
